resume:
DANIEL VOSS
Junior Data Analyst | Brooklyn, NY | daniel.voss.data@email.com

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Data analyst with two years of hands-on experience turning messy operational data into dashboards and reports that operators actually use. I started in a customer-operations role and moved into analytics by automating the reports my own team relied on, which means I tend to think about the human on the other end of the chart, not just the SQL. I am most comfortable in the SQL-to-dashboard layer: writing queries against a warehouse, modeling them into clean tables, and building visualizations in Looker and Tableau. I have working Python for ad-hoc analysis (pandas, matplotlib) and I am steadily improving my statistics. I care a lot about data quality, because most of my early mistakes came from trusting numbers I had not validated.

EXPERIENCE

Data Analyst — FreshCart (grocery delivery startup), Jan 2024 to Present
FreshCart is a same-day grocery delivery company operating in six metro areas. I sit on the operations analytics team, which supports the dispatch, courier, and warehouse functions. My day-to-day is roughly half scheduled reporting and half ad-hoc requests from operations managers who need a number to make a staffing or routing decision.
- Rebuilt the daily operations dashboard in Looker after the previous version had drifted out of sync with the warehouse schema. I interviewed the three operations managers who used it to understand which tiles they actually looked at, cut the dashboard from forty tiles to twelve, and re-pointed every metric at validated source tables. Dashboard load time dropped from roughly forty seconds to under five, and the ops managers stopped maintaining their own shadow spreadsheets.
- Built a courier-idle-time analysis that broke down where couriers were waiting during shifts (at the store, between batches, at the customer door). I joined dispatch logs to GPS pings and surfaced that a single understaffed store was responsible for a large share of in-store wait. Operations used it to re-balance courier assignments.
- Wrote and scheduled the SQL models behind the weekly courier-pay report, which had previously been assembled by hand in spreadsheets every Monday. Automating it removed a recurring several-hour manual task and cut the pay-dispute rate because the numbers stopped depending on who built the sheet.
- Set up basic data-quality checks (row-count thresholds, null checks on key columns, freshness checks) that alert the team in Slack when an upstream pipeline silently breaks. Before this, we usually found out a feed was broken when a manager complained that a dashboard looked wrong.
- Partnered with the data engineering team to document the dispatch event tables, which were previously tribal knowledge. I wrote the first column-level documentation so new analysts could self-serve.

Customer Operations Associate — FreshCart, Jun 2023 to Jan 2024
I joined FreshCart on the operations floor handling escalations from couriers and customers. This is where I learned the data from the bottom up — I knew what a 'failed delivery' really meant operationally before I ever queried the table for it.
- Handled live courier escalations during peak dinner hours, coordinating with dispatch to re-route stuck orders.
- Noticed that a recurring class of refund requests traced back to a mislabeled store-hours field, and I documented the pattern well enough that engineering fixed the root cause. This is the project that got me moved onto the analytics team.
- Built my first real SQL queries here, initially just to answer my own team's questions faster than waiting on the analytics queue.
- Sat in on the dispatch team's daily standup for six months, which is where I learned which metrics operators trust and which ones they quietly ignore because they have been burned by a bad number before.
- Wrote a one-page runbook for the most common operational questions so other associates could answer them without escalating, which cut the volume of questions hitting the analytics queue.

SELECTED PROJECTS
Courier-onboarding funnel analysis (2024): operations suspected we were losing new couriers somewhere between application and first delivery, but nobody could say where. I stitched together the applicant tracker, the background-check vendor export, and the first-delivery timestamp from the dispatch logs into a single funnel table. The drop-off turned out to be concentrated at the background-check step, where a slow vendor turnaround was causing applicants to go cold. I quantified the stage-by-stage conversion and the median days spent at each step. Operations used the analysis to renegotiate the vendor SLA and to add an automated nudge to applicants stuck in the check queue. The lesson I took from it: the interesting number is almost never the top-line conversion rate, it is the step where the rate falls off a cliff.
Weekend-surge staffing model (2024): the warehouse team kept either over- or under-staffing Saturday mornings. I pulled two years of order-volume data, decomposed it into weekday baseline plus weekend lift plus weather effect, and built a simple lookup the shift planners could use the Thursday before to set Saturday headcount. It was deliberately not a fancy model — the planners told me they would not trust a black box, so I kept it transparent enough that they could see exactly why it suggested a given number. Adoption was the whole point, and they actually used it.

INTERNSHIP
Marketing Data Intern — Northbeam Media, Summer 2022
- Pulled campaign performance data and built weekly summary decks for the marketing team.
- Learned the basics of attribution and why marketing numbers and finance numbers never quite match.

EDUCATION
B.S. Statistics, Rutgers University, 2023
Relevant coursework: regression analysis, probability, database systems, intro to machine learning.

TECHNICAL SKILLS
SQL (strong — window functions, CTEs, query optimization on a Redshift warehouse), Looker (LookML modeling), Tableau, Python (pandas, matplotlib, intermediate), dbt (basic), Git (basic), Excel (advanced). Comfortable with star-schema modeling and with debugging why two dashboards disagree.

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
I want to go deeper on the analytics-engineering side — more dbt, more rigorous testing, more ownership of the modeling layer rather than only the dashboard layer. I learn fastest when I am close to the operators using the data, and I want to keep that closeness while leveling up the technical craft.
jd: Acuity Logistics is hiring a Junior Data Analyst for our supply-chain analytics team. You will work alongside operations and data-engineering partners to turn warehouse, routing, and labor data into reporting and analysis that drives daily operational decisions. Core responsibilities: write and maintain SQL models against our cloud warehouse, build and own dashboards in Looker or Tableau, investigate data-quality issues and partner with engineering on fixes, and translate ambiguous operator questions into concrete analyses. The ideal candidate has 1-3 years of experience, strong SQL, comfort with at least one BI tool, and — most importantly — a demonstrated instinct for whether a number can be trusted. We value analysts who validate before they publish and who can explain a metric to a non-technical operations manager. Python and dbt are pluses. This role reports to the Analytics Manager and partners daily with three operations leads.

history.q: Tell me about a time you didn't trust a number you were about to publish, what made you suspicious, and what you did about it before it went out.

candidate_last_answer: Yeah, this one stuck with me. The Situation: I was about to ship a new courier-utilization tile on our ops dashboard, and the number it showed was 94 percent utilization, which would have been fantastic and also way too good for our network. My Task was to either confirm it or kill it before the ops managers saw it in Monday standup. The Action: I traced the metric back through the LookML and found the denominator was only counting couriers who had logged at least one delivery that day, so anyone who clocked in but sat idle the whole shift was silently dropped — that's exactly the population that drags utilization down. I rebuilt the denominator off the shift-clock table instead of the delivery table, added a row-count check comparing the two sources, and re-ran it against three historical weeks to make sure the new number tracked our payroll hours. The Result: real utilization came out at 71 percent, I shipped that instead, and I added a freshness-plus-rowcount alert so the denominator can't silently shrink again. The ops lead later told me she'd almost set a staffing target off the bad 94 figure, so catching it before Monday is the part I'm proud of.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: re-ran it against three historical weeks to make sure the new number tracked our payroll hours

expected-yield: the precise validation mechanism — how closely the rebuilt utilization matched payroll hours week over week, what tolerance counted as a match, and what he would have done if two of the three weeks had disagreed
